login.brand_tagline: Your AI training partner for triathlon. Built on top of Intervals.icu.
login.legal_1: By signing in you accept the terms & privacy policy.
login.legal_2: Endurai is independent — not affiliated with Intervals.icu.

endurance.tooltip: One composite score for your endurance state across all sports. Zones: Detrained → Recovering → Maintaining → Productive → Peaking. Productive and Peaking are the target states for race-day shape.

load.no_swim_data: No swim sessions found in this period

tip.decoupling: How steady your heart rate stays on long easy efforts. We take your last few long sessions: under 5% — strong aerobic base; 5–10% — watch fuelling and hydration; over 10% — fatigue or a weak base.
tip.zones: How your training time splits across intensity zones. Mostly easy plus a little hard work (polarized) is the goal. Too much medium-hard "grey zone" leaves you always a bit tired but never sharp.
tip.ef: Efficiency Factor — speed (or power) per heartbeat. When it rises you're doing more work for the same effort: the aerobic engine is getting stronger. A single week is noisy — watch the multi-week trend.
tip.drift: Each dot is one session's heart-rate drift — how much HR crept up for the same effort. Green — healthy. Amber — watch hydration and pacing. Red — fatigue, dehydration, or a fading aerobic base.
tip.bike_readiness: Three signals that together say if you're bike-ready for the chosen distance: Volume (fitness vs target), Long ride (your longest recent ride), and Durability (how well HR holds on long rides). All three green — ready.
tip.marathon_shape: An overall score of how race-ready you are for the chosen road distance — from fitness, long-run capacity and durability. The predicted time comes from your training paces; its range narrows as you log more races.

history.title: All history
history.subtitle: {{days}} days of recovery · tap a day for its snapshot

wellness.desktop_subtitle: Recovery, sleep & training load — refreshed every morning
wellness.skip_override: HRV red — skip today regardless of score
wellness.breakdown_note: 4 weighted inputs combined. Modifiers: HRV CV > 15% → −5pts. Late sleep (after 23:00) → −10pts.

activities.title: Activity
activities.no_details: No detailed data for this activity.
activities.open_planned_workout: Open the planned workout this activity executed

tip.plan_vs_actual: Plan vs actual comparison. Δ shows duration and load drift. Compliance ≥ 95% — on plan, 80–95% — close, below — off-plan.
tip.hr_zones: Time spent in each heart-rate zone (% of total duration). Z1–Z2 — aerobic base, Z3 — tempo, Z4 — threshold, Z5+ — VO₂max and above. Zones are computed from your LTHR.
tip.ef: Efficiency Factor — power or pace per heart-rate unit. Rising means your aerobic system is more efficient. Compare across similar Z2 sessions.
tip.decoupling: How much your heart rate drifted at the same power towards the end. < 5% — strong aerobic base, 5–10% — normal, > 10% — under-recovered or session too hard.
tip.if: Intensity Factor — average intensity relative to threshold (Intervals reports it as a percent). ~75 ≈ endurance, ~85 ≈ tempo, > 100 — above FTP / LTHR.
tip.avg_hr: Average heart rate for the session; «peak» is the maximum reached during this session (not your physiological max HR from settings). Warm-up and cool-down drag the average down.
tip.ess: TRIMP-equivalent — HR-based training stress. 100 ≈ 1 hour at LTHR. TSS counterpart: TSS is power/pace-based, ESS is HR-based.
tip.tss: Training Stress Score — total session load relative to your threshold. 100 ≈ 1 hour at FTP / LTHR. Computed from power or pace. ESS is the HR-based counterpart.

dfa.ra: Readiness (Ra) — DFA α1 readiness: how stable your aerobic threshold is at low HR vs your own baseline. «+» — above base, «−» — under-recovered. ±5% is the noise band, not a signal.
dfa.da: Durability (Da) — DFA α1 durability: how well the threshold holds **toward the end** of the session. «+» — preserved under fatigue, «−» — fell apart (weak aerobic base or session too hard).
dfa.hrvt1: First aerobic threshold by DFA α1 (≈ LT1 / Z2 ceiling). HR, power, pace where the system stops «running easy». Volume sessions should stay below HRVT1.
dfa.hrvt2: Second threshold by DFA α1 (≈ LT2 / Z3-Z4). HR where aerobics start to break down. Tempo and threshold intervals cluster around HRVT2.
dfa.dfa_mean: Average DFA α1 for the session. > 1.0 — low intensity (Z1-Z2), ≈ 0.75 — near LT1 / HRVT1, < 0.5 — above LT2 / HRVT2.
dfa.quality: HRV signal quality for the session (Excellent / Good / Poor). Computed from the share of clean RR intervals. More noise → less reliable Ra/Da/HRVT1/HRVT2 above.

onboarding.connect_intro: Endurai runs on top of Intervals.icu — your existing training log. We need read/write access to plan workouts, track wellness, and analyze trends.
onboarding.scope_settings: Update FTP / LTHR / CSS when they change
onboarding.privacy: Your data stays in your Intervals.icu account. We never store raw activity files — we read on demand.

weekly.title: Weekly reports
weekly.empty: No weekly reports yet. The first one will arrive Sunday evening.
weekly.not_found: No report saved for this week.
weekly.written_by: Written every Monday by your coach AI.

coach.no_note: No coach note for today yet.
coach.no_note_date: No coach note for this day.

hrv.metric: HRV — heart-rate variability at morning rest. Higher means your nervous system recovered better. Compared against YOUR own 7- and 60-day averages, not «what athlete X has» — your personal baseline is what matters.
hrv.trend: Each point is your daily value. Don't read a single day — read the shape: a downward slope over 3+ weeks means dial back the load or check your health. A sharp one-day dip is usually noise.
hrv.stats: Mean 7 and 60 days — your short and long baselines. SWC is the smallest meaningful change; anything smaller is noise. CV — how stable HRV is over a week (stability matters more than a one-off peak). Trend — where the baseline is heading over recent weeks.

rhr.metric: RHR — resting pulse in the morning. Lower means your heart works more efficiently at rest. A 5+ bpm jump above normal can hint at fatigue, poor sleep, or an upcoming illness. Compared against YOUR 7- and 30-day average.
rhr.trend: Each point is your daily value. Steadily below baseline — your heart is adapting, good sign. Several days in a row above — listen to your body, you may need an easy week.
rhr.stats: Mean 7, 30 and 60 days — short and long baselines. Bounds — your usual range (±1 SD). CV — week-over-week stability. Trend — where the baseline is heading.

meaning.excellent: Recovery high — system ready to load. {{goodPct}}% of last {{days}} days in the green zone.
meaning.good: Recovery normal. {{goodPct}}% of last {{days}} days scored above 70. Train as planned.
meaning.moderate: Score moderate — {{avg}} on average. Mind the intensity; planned Z2 is fine.
meaning.low: Recovery low. Rest or Z1 needed; {{lowPct}}% of last {{days}} days in the red zone.
meaning.no_today: No data for today. Average score over {{days}} days: {{avg}}.

body_trend.title: Body trend
body_trend.subtitle: Weight · body fat · VO₂max · steps · last {{days}} days

tip.ctl_atl: CTL (Fitness) is your 42-day average training load — your aerobic base. It builds slowly and fades slowly. ATL (Fatigue) is your 7-day average — fresh tiredness from recent sessions. When the red line (ATL) climbs above the blue (CTL) you're in a build block, accumulating fatigue faster than you're adapting. When they converge again, you've recovered.
tip.tsb: TSB (Form) = CTL − ATL: the gap between your base and your fresh fatigue. It tells you how ready you are for hard work or racing. −10 to +5 is normal working range. Below −30 means deep fatigue, risk of overtraining. +5 to +25 is fresh — race-day window. Above +25 means you're losing fitness.

personal_edit.subtitle: Age, weight and per-sport max HR. Only age is editable today.
